entries.description: Apples within 50 ft of mature English walnut show measurable canopy decline and yield loss attributed to juglone. Plant orchard blocks upslope or upstream of any walnut row.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; Penn State Tree Fruit

entries.description: Asparagus shows moderate juglone sensitivity. Locate the asparagus bed at least 50 ft from English walnut; closer plantings show stalk wilt and reduced crown vigor over several years.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Brambles are highly juglone-sensitive. Plant raspberries a minimum of 50 ft from mature English walnut to avoid cane decline. Susceptibility is symptomatic of soil juglone accumulation rather than visible mature canes.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Brassicas grown within the English walnut root zone exhibit slow growth and yellowing, attributed to juglone interference with respiration. Maintain a 40 ft buffer to keep cabbages productive.
entries.source: Penn State Extension; USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Brambles are exceptionally juglone-sensitive; black walnut juglone suppresses raspberry cane growth at distances greater than for English walnut. Maintain at least a 70 ft buffer; longer if mature trees are present.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Asparagus crown decline near black walnut is well documented; juglone disrupts crown vigor within the root drip zone. Maintain a minimum 60 ft buffer; replanting in former-walnut soil requires multi-year remediation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Pecan produces juglone at lower concentrations than walnut but enough to suppress sensitive nightshades within 30-40 ft of mature trees. Plant tomato beds a minimum of 50 ft from mature pecans.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; UF/IFAS Extension pecan

entries.description: Apples within 40 ft of mature pecan trees show modest canopy decline attributed to low-level juglone exposure. Effects are less severe than walnut but worth a 50 ft buffer in mixed-tree orchards.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: White-clover living mulch between blueberry rows fixes nitrogen and tolerates the acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5) that blueberries require, where most legumes fail. Mow in alternate strips for continuous bloom.
entries.source: OSU Extension blueberry production; USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Sage blooms during the blueberry petal-fall window and draws bumblebees, which are far more effective at blueberry pollination than honeybees because of buzz pollination. Plant a few sage clumps at row ends.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Xerces Society pollinator guide

entries.description: Creeping thyme between blueberry rows tolerates acidic soil, suppresses weeds, and provides a long-bloom nectar source for native bees. The dense mat reduces splash dispersal of soil-borne pathogens onto low fruit.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Borage blooms during the strawberry runner-set window and draws bumblebees and honeybees, improving fruit-set rates measurably. Refilling nectar every two minutes keeps borage as a continuous forage station.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Sage flowers near strawberry beds support native bees and bumblebees during fruit set. The strong sage aroma also discourages strawberry root weevil oviposition.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Creeping thyme between strawberry rows suppresses slug populations and reduces splash dispersal of gray mold (Botrytis) onto low fruit. Tolerant of strawberry's slightly acidic soil.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension small fruit guide; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Tansy at raspberry row ends deters Japanese beetle and raspberry cane borer adults. Cut hard before seed maturity; tansy escapes readily through orchard floors.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Garlic interplanted with raspberries reduces aphid colonization and discourages some nematode species. Harvest each summer; replant from saved bulbs in autumn.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension small fruit guide

entries.description: Tansy clumps adjacent to blackberry rows deter Japanese beetle, which heavily damages tender canes and ripening fruit. Cut tansy before seed maturity; spreads aggressively through brambles.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Holy basil (tulsi) releases methyl eugenol and linalool volatiles that repel thrips and whitefly from tomatoes, the same mechanism as culinary basil but with a stronger aromatic profile and reliable perennial-like growth in zones 9 and warmer. Plant in a continuous row alongside tomato beds.
entries.source: ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard tulsi: Rama tulsi (Ocimum sanctum, green-leafed cultivar) carries the strongest eugenol concentration and best repels thrips and whitefly in tomato beds. Treat as a tender annual outside zone 9.
entries.source: ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Tulsi volatiles repel aphids and thrips from pepper plants. Interplant within rows for full season coverage; the strong aroma also confuses pepper weevil oviposition during fruit set.
entries.source: ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research

entries.description: Tulsi planted at the eggplant row edges deters flea beetle by masking the host-finding volatiles eggplant releases. Pair with floating row cover during early flea beetle pressure for layered protection.
entries.source: ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is a nightshade-family medicinal that shares some pest pressures with tomato but also contains withanolides that deter aphids and spider mites. Plant at row ends, not interplanted, to avoid late blight cross-host concerns.
entries.source: ICAR-National Bureau of Plant Genetic Resources; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Calendula's long bloom supports pollinators throughout ashwagandha's late-summer flowering window, supporting seed set on this annual-cycled medicinal. Pair as a low-growing edge plant.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Echinacea and yarrow form a long-bloom perennial pair for predator and parasitoid habitat. Combined plantings support resident populations of hoverflies, parasitic wasps, and lacewings across the full growing season.
entries.source: Xerces Society conservation biological control guide; USDA-NRCS

entries.description: Echinacea and bee balm bloom in overlapping mid-to-late-summer windows, with bee balm's tubular flowers favoring hummingbirds and bumblebees while echinacea draws native solitary bees and butterflies. A standard pollinator-garden pairing.
entries.source: Xerces Society pollinator guide; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Echinacea bordering tomato beds supplies long-bloom pollinator habitat that builds resident parasitic-wasp populations. Wasps move into tomato rows to attack tomato hornworm and tomato fruitworm larvae.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Echinacea draws braconid and ichneumon wasps that parasitize cabbage worm and diamondback moth larvae on broccoli. Plant as a perennial border to brassica beds for season-long parasitoid support.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: echinacea borders support parasitic wasp populations that attack cabbage worm and diamondback moth larvae. A perennial-edge alternative to annual companion flowers.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Valerian's umbel-like clusters draw earthworms toward the root zone and attract parasitic flies and wasps that target tomato fruitworm. Plant in a corner of the tomato bed; one plant covers a 12 ft row.
entries.source: Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: valerian supports beneficial insect habitat. Particularly useful at brassica bed corners where its phosphorus-cycling roots improve nutrient availability.
entries.source: Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Feverfew's pyrethrin content repels carrot rust fly and aphids. Plant a continuous border at carrot bed edges; cut hard before seed set to keep feverfew from naturalizing.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Feverfew's natural pyrethrin compounds repel aphids and discourage cabbage moth oviposition. Plant in continuous edge rows along brassica beds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Feverfew at lettuce row edges reduces aphid colonization and discourages leafhopper feeding. Pair with sweet alyssum for combined hoverfly and pyrethrin coverage.
entries.source: Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Milk thistle's deep taproot mines subsoil minerals and aerates compacted soil under rose hips. Chop-and-drop before seed set; let some seed each year for self-renewing soil-builder cover.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Mullein's tall flowering spike attracts pollinators across a long bloom window and provides a vertical perch for predatory wasps and assassin bugs. Biennial; replace plants every two years for continuous coverage.
entries.source: Xerces Society; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Nettle accumulates nitrogen, iron, and silica in its foliage; chop-and-drop or steep as tea for foliar spray boosts tomato growth and disease resistance. Plant in a contained corner; nettle spreads aggressively via rhizome.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Toensmeier, Perennial Vegetables

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: nettle foliage applied as mulch or tea around broccoli supplies bioavailable nitrogen and silica. Contain growth with deep edging.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Nettle tea applied as a soil drench to strawberry beds increases vigor and fruit set; the silica content also reduces gray mold susceptibility. Use sparingly; strawberry has shallow roots and is sensitive to over-fertilization.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Toensmeier, Perennial Vegetables

entries.description: Calendula hosts aphid populations that draw resident ladybugs and lacewings into brassica beds. The bright bloom also confuses cabbage white butterflies during oviposition scouting.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Calendula's long bloom from spring through frost attracts hoverflies, ladybugs, and parasitic wasps. The medicinal cultivars carry higher resin content than ornamental forms, with no impact on tomato pollinator function.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Calendula borders around pepper beds support hoverfly populations that prey on pepper aphid. The long bloom outlasts most annual companion flowers and reseeds without effort.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: German chamomile draws hoverflies and beneficial wasps that target tomato hornworm and aphids. Chamomile tea applied as a foliar spray is also a traditional damping-off preventive for seedlings.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Chamomile's umbel-like flower clusters support parasitic wasps that target cabbage worm larvae. Tradition holds that chamomile improves brassica flavor, though the mechanism is unverified.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Chamomile bloom timing overlaps cucumber flower production and draws pollinators needed for female-flower fruit set. Easy reseeding annual.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Narrow-leaf echinacea and bee balm bloom in overlapping windows for hummingbirds, bumblebees, and native bees. A standard prairie-medicinal pollinator hub planting.
entries.source: Xerces Society pollinator guide; USDA-NRCS

entries.description: Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) flowers attract small native bees and parasitic wasps; pairing with yarrow extends the predator-supporting bloom window across the season.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: St. John's wort flowers in June and supports native bee diversity; paired with yarrow it forms a low-maintenance medicinal-pollinator hedge edge. Plant on the south-facing edge of a garden for best bloom.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Passionflower's complex flower structure favors large bees (carpenter, bumblebee) for pollination; nearby yarrow patches sustain pollinator populations between passionflower bloom flushes.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Astragalus is a nitrogen-fixing legume genus; the medicinal Astragalus membranaceus contributes N to surrounding perennial medicinal beds while echinacea handles the pollinator function. A standard medicinal-bed pairing.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; ICAR medicinal plants research

entries.description: Wine cap (Stropharia rugosoannulata) cultivated in wood-chip mulched lettuce paths breaks down lignin and releases plant-available nutrients to lettuce root zones. The mycelium also retains moisture in the top inch of soil; lettuce yields improve in concert.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program mushroom cultivation

entries.description: Wine cap mycelium in wood-chip paths between brassica beds supplies a slow nitrogen release during decomposition and increases mycorrhizal colonization of nearby crops. Refresh wood-chip layer each spring.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: wine cap colonizes wood-chip mulch around cabbage beds and improves soil biology while suppressing pathogen-supporting fungi via competitive exclusion.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Wine cap mycelium in pathway wood chips near kale beds provides continuous mulch breakdown and moisture retention through the long kale season. Pairs well with permanent-bed designs.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Wine cap in wood-chip mulched tomato pathways supports moisture retention and slow nutrient release. The mycelial network has been shown to scavenge bacterial wilt and Phytophthora propagules in laboratory studies.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; USDA-ARS mycoremediation research

entries.description: Wine cap fruits prolifically in straw-mulched winter squash beds, particularly under wide-spaced vine canopies that retain humidity. The mycelium improves nutrient release as the squash bed transitions through summer.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.description: Hazelnut wood and live tree shade make ideal placement for shiitake logs in dappled-shade understory. Logs benefit from 60-80 percent shade and high humidity beneath the hazelnut canopy without competing for vertical space.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program mushroom cultivation; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Red alder coppice produces ideal shiitake substrate (hardwood, 4-6 inch diameter) and provides understory shade for log inoculation operations. A coppiced alder grove can supply continuous mushroom substrate and timber.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; OSU Extension forest products

entries.description: Sugar maple makes excellent shiitake substrate with a 2-4 year fruiting window per log. Inoculate logs cut during winter dormancy; arrange in dappled shade beneath canopy maples for natural humidity.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; University of Vermont Extension

entries.description: Pearl oyster mushroom blocks fruiting in shaded lettuce beds add a secondary harvest layer without competing for soil resources. The mycelium does not enter lettuce root zones; cultivation is contained to inoculated substrate.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.description: Same mechanism as lettuce: shade-tolerant pearl oyster blocks pair well with cool-season spinach beds. The two crops occupy non-competing layers and benefit from shared shade and moisture.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Lion's mane fruiting blocks placed in deep-shade corners near kale beds add a high-value mushroom layer to a vegetable garden. The mushroom does not interact with kale roots; placement is for shade and humidity coverage.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Wine cap in pine-needle and wood-chip mulched blueberry beds tolerates the acidic soil and contributes to mulch breakdown. Pair with leaf-mold mulch refreshes each year.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Wine cap mycelium in wood-chip mulched raspberry rows supports moisture retention and slow nutrient release. The mushrooms fruit in the year-two refreshed mulch layer.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Wine cap in wood-chip mulched orchard alleys breaks down lignin and contributes to slow soil-building. The mycelium has been shown to support apple-tree fine-root function in mycorrhizal studies.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Cover-crop residue from winter rye, mowed and left as mulch on permanent beds, supports excellent wine cap fruiting the following year. The mycelium breaks down rye allelopathic compounds during decomposition, freeing the bed for sensitive crops.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Buckwheat planted at tomato row ends blooms in 4-6 weeks and supplies a high-nectar pollen source for hoverflies and parasitic wasps that target tomato hornworm and aphids. Mow before seed set; reseed for a second flush.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Buckwheat between brassica plantings serves as a fast pollinator and beneficial-insect insectary while breaking the disease cycle. Six-week window from sowing to mow-down clears the bed for fall brassica seeding.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: buckwheat in alternating brassica beds builds parasitoid populations. Particularly useful between spring and fall cabbage plantings for a fast rotation crop.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Buckwheat in adjacent rows draws hoverflies whose larvae prey on carrot aphid. The dense flower head also supplies nectar to parasitic wasps targeting carrot rust fly.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Hairy vetch overwintered and rolled before tomato transplant supplies 90-200 lb N/acre. Roll-killed vetch mulch in place also suppresses early blight splash dispersal and conserves soil moisture through July heat.
entries.source: USDA-ARS hairy vetch research; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Hairy vetch roll-killed in place provides peppers with steady N release and reduces transplant shock through cooler soil temperatures. The mulch layer suppresses bacterial spot splash dispersal.
entries.source: USDA-ARS hairy vetch research

entries.description: Hairy vetch overwintered and rolled before squash transplant supplies N and forms a no-till mulch that vines crawl across. Squash benefits from cooler soil and reduced weed competition during establishment.
entries.source: USDA-ARS hairy vetch research; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Crimson clover overwintered before sweet corn supplies 70-130 lb N/acre and supports pollinator habitat during the early-season corn window. Mow at bloom-end and incorporate or leave as residue.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Winter rye sown in fall and mowed in spring before corn supplies a no-till mulch that suppresses weeds, conserves moisture, and reduces corn rootworm pressure. Best paired with vetch for combined N and biomass.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Iowa State Extension

entries.description: Winter rye residue releases hydroxamic acids (BX compounds) that inhibit germination of small-seeded crops including lettuce. Wait at least 3 weeks after rye incorporation before direct-seeding lettuce.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Rye allelochemicals suppress carrot germination and early seedling growth. Wait 3-4 weeks after rye incorporation, or follow rye with a fast-growing buffer crop (buckwheat) before sowing carrots.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Onion seedlings are highly sensitive to rye BX-compound residue. Allow 4+ weeks after rye mow-down before direct seeding, or transplant onion sets rather than seed in former rye beds.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Spinach is among the most rye-sensitive small-seeded vegetables. Plant a rye-free buffer crop (buckwheat or oats) between winter rye and direct-seeded spinach to clear allelochemicals.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Hairy vetch interseeded with winter wheat at heading provides 60-100 lb N/acre after wheat harvest, reducing fertilizer needs for the following crop. Wheat canopy suppresses vetch enough to keep harvest clean.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Penn State Extension small grains

entries.description: Frost-seeded red clover into winter wheat in early spring establishes a nitrogen-fixing understory that emerges after wheat harvest. Provides 80-150 lb N/acre for the next crop and supports pollinator habitat.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Iowa State Extension

entries.description: Spring oats and field pea co-seeded form a classic biomass-plus-nitrogen pair. Oats serves as a nurse crop for the pea; the combination produces 4-6 tons biomass per acre and 40-80 lb N/acre.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Oats serves as a nurse crop for Austrian winter pea, suppressing weeds while pea establishes. Winterkilled in cold zones; supplies 60-120 lb N/acre for the following crop in mild climates where pea overwinters.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Berseem clover co-seeded with oats in spring produces a high-biomass, high-N pairing for incorporation 6-8 weeks later. Berseem outperforms red clover in dry summer climates.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Sorghum-sudangrass and cowpea co-seeded in summer fix N while building 6-8 tons biomass per acre. The combination is the most productive warm-season cover-crop pair for soil-building rotations.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Sunn hemp and sorghum-sudangrass co-seeded for a heavy summer biomass and nitrogen pulse. Sunn hemp also suppresses root-knot nematode, complementing sorghum-sudan's biofumigant residue.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Buckwheat at sweet corn row ends supplies hoverfly and parasitic wasp habitat that targets corn earworm and aphids. Sow 4 weeks before corn silking for peak bloom coincidence.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Three Sisters classic: flint corn provides the tall, sturdy structure pole beans climb. Flint corn's denser stem outperforms sweet corn varieties for bean support; harvest beans through corn maturation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Three Sisters research; Iroquois agricultural tradition

entries.description: Three Sisters classic: winter squash vines through the corn-bean mound suppress weeds and conserve soil moisture. Flint corn varieties tolerate the squash canopy better than shorter sweet corn.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Three Sisters research; Iroquois agricultural tradition

entries.description: Three Sisters classic: pole beans fix atmospheric nitrogen via Rhizobium and release some to companion flint corn during the season. The Iroquois-developed cultivars are bred for this synergy.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Three Sisters research

entries.description: Dent corn (Zea mays var. indentata) provides equally strong support to pole beans as flint corn, with greater grain yield for the gardener. Bean nodulation supplies N at the cost of slight competition for moisture.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Three Sisters research

entries.description: Three Sisters variant: dent corn rows over-canopy winter squash vines, suppressing weeds and conserving moisture. The mound design supports drainage during heavy summer rain.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Three Sisters research

entries.description: Spring wheat undersown with red clover at the 4-leaf stage provides a nurse-crop relationship where wheat suppresses weeds while clover establishes. After wheat harvest, clover supplies N for the following crop.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Iowa State Extension

entries.description: Same nurse-crop pattern as wheat: barley undersown with red clover at the 4-leaf stage. Particularly common in cool-climate dairy rotations where the clover supplies forage and N after barley harvest.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Rye and hairy vetch overwintered together combine deep biomass with high nitrogen yield. Rye scavenges residual N to prevent leaching; vetch fixes new N. The pair builds soil organic matter and supplies the next crop.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Same pairing as rye-grain: winter rye cover with hairy vetch combines biomass and N. Roll-killed mulch in spring supplies a heavy no-till residue for warm-season transplants.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Tepary bean and amaranth grain co-cropped in arid systems are a Southwest US heritage pairing. Tepary fixes nitrogen for amaranth while sharing drought tolerance; both crops mature in the same window for harvest.
entries.source: Native Seeds/SEARCH; University of Arizona Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Quinoa and lupini (Andean lupine) co-cropped in highland traditional systems supply both staple grain and pulse from the same plot. Lupini fixes N for quinoa over a single season; cultivars co-evolved in the central Andes.
entries.source: CIP-International Potato Center; FAO crop diversification publications

entries.description: Pearl millet and cowpea intercropped is a West African dryland staple. Cowpea fixes 40-80 lb N/acre during the millet season; the legume mulch suppresses weeds beneath the millet canopy.
entries.source: ICRISAT dryland systems research; FAO

entries.description: Same mechanism as pearl millet: cowpea intercropped with sorghum supplies N and weed suppression. The deep-rooted cowpea taps subsoil moisture that supports both crops in dryland conditions.
entries.source: ICRISAT dryland systems research

entries.description: Azolla (a floating fern with cyanobacterial symbiont) fixes 30-100 kg N/ha in paddy systems; in upland rice it forms a moisture-retaining living mulch when soil stays consistently damp. Not a substitute for traditional companions but supplemental.
entries.source: IRRI rice production; FAO Azolla research

entries.description: Teff undersown with red clover supplies N and biomass while the fine-stemmed teff grain develops. Common in Ethiopian highland rotations; pair tolerates a wide range of soils.
entries.source: FAO Africa Soil Health Consortium; Ethiopian Institute of Agricultural Research

entries.description: Sunflower allelopathy via sesquiterpene lactones inhibits potato sprouting and early growth. Maintain at least 15 ft separation between sunflower rows and the potato bed; do not follow sunflower with potato in rotation without a full season buffer.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; OSU Extension

entries.description: Sunflower residue suppresses carrot germination and early growth. Wait 4-6 weeks after sunflower removal before direct seeding carrots; or rotate through a buckwheat buffer.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Sunflower sesquiterpenes inhibit lettuce germination. Allow 6+ weeks between sunflower mow-down and direct lettuce seeding; or transplant lettuce starts to avoid germination phase entirely.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Sunflower residue contains allelochemicals that suppress wheat seedling vigor for one full season. Avoid rotating sunflower directly to winter wheat; allow a fall buckwheat or oats buffer.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; Kansas State Extension

entries.description: Phacelia tanacetifolia draws honeybees and bumblebees during the cucumber flowering window, increasing female-flower fruit set rates. The 6-week bloom can be staggered with second seeding.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as cucumber: phacelia near squash beds draws bumblebees during morning pollination windows. Squash is pollinator-limited; phacelia improves observable fruit set.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research

entries.description: Same mechanism as winter squash and cucumber: phacelia supplies a high-density pollinator-forage station that improves zucchini and summer squash fruit set rates.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research

entries.description: Phacelia in adjacent rows supplies a high-nectar pollen source for hoverflies whose larvae prey on tomato aphids. The fast 6-week bloom can be reseeded for season-long predator habitat.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as phacelia: the lacy-phacelia cultivar form has the same hoverfly draw with somewhat extended bloom duration. Plant in narrow border strips along tomato rows.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Phacelia bloom timing supports parasitic wasps targeting carrot rust fly during the spring emergence window. Plant in continuous border strips at carrot bed edges.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Garlic planted in a ring at rose root zones reduces blackspot disease pressure and discourages aphid colonization. Traditional rose-garden practice; modern research confirms garlic sulfur volatiles reduce fungal spore germination on adjacent leaves.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Chive at the rose base reduces aphid colonization and supplies a long-bloom early-spring pollinator attractor for native bees. The classic rose-and-chive border.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Buckwheat sown after winter wheat harvest in summer fills the rotation gap, suppresses weeds, and supplies fast biomass for incorporation before fall planting. Standard small-grain rotation tool.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Penn State Extension small grains

entries.description: Same mechanism as wheat: buckwheat fills the summer gap after oat harvest. The fast bloom also supports pollinator habitat between cereal cycles.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Buckwheat following rye harvest scavenges allelochemicals and prepares the bed for sensitive small-seeded crops. Six-week buckwheat cycle clears rye residue effects without herbicide.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Genovese basil's methyl eugenol and linalool repel whitefly and thrips from San Marzano paste tomato vines. The traditional pairing of Campanian sauce-making, with documented flavor synergy in shared root-zone volatiles.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; ISHS basil-tomato volatile research

entries.description: Same mechanism as San Marzano: basil volatiles repel whitefly and thrips from Roma paste tomatoes. The determinate growth of Roma means basil at 18 inch spacing maintains coverage through full vine height.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Indeterminate Italian heirloom tomatoes (Costoluto, Cuore di Bue, etc.) benefit from continuous basil borders. Vine canopy reaches 6 ft; plant basil between vines at 12 inch spacing for season-long volatile coverage.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Thai basil's stronger eugenol profile suits cherry tomato's small-fruit dense canopy. The compact basil habit fits between cherry tomato cages without crowding; trellising both maintains airflow.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Standard sweet basil interplanted with cherry tomatoes repels whitefly and supplies pollinator habitat. Cherry tomato's smaller leaf canopy lets more sunlight reach basil, supporting heavier basil leaf production than paste-tomato beds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as cherry tomato: basil volatiles deter whitefly and thrips. Black Cherry's indeterminate vine reaches 6 ft; plant basil at 12 inch spacing along the row for canopy coverage.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Arkansas Traveler is heat-tolerant and pairs well with the heat-loving Thai basil. The combination thrives in southern US summers where Genovese basil bolts prematurely.
entries.source: University of Arkansas Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as Arkansas Traveler: heat-tolerant Creole pairs with Thai basil through humid southern summers. Both crops tolerate high humidity better than the standard Mediterranean tomato-basil pairing.
entries.source: LSU AgCenter; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard tulsi: Rama tulsi tolerates heat and humidity that breaks down Genovese basil. Ideal companion for gulf-coast tomato beds where Mediterranean herbs struggle.
entries.source: LSU AgCenter; ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research

entries.description: Signet marigold (Tagetes tenuifolia) interplanted with San Marzano vines suppresses root-knot nematode while bordering the bed with citrus-edible flowers. Smaller habit than French marigold; fits in row gaps.
entries.source: USDA-ARS nematode research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as San Marzano: signet marigold deters root-knot nematode and supplies edible flowers for the kitchen. Suits indeterminate heirloom beds where French marigold takes too much space.
entries.source: USDA-ARS nematode research

entries.description: French and African marigolds in the Tagetes genus produce alpha-terthienyl in roots, which suppresses root-knot nematode populations. Effect strongest with continuous marigold cover for one full season prior to tomato planting.
entries.source: USDA-ARS root-knot nematode research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Common milkweed near tomato beds hosts oleander aphid (Aphis nerii) colonies that draw resident ladybug and lacewing populations. The predator buildup spills over onto tomato aphid pressure. Plant in a contained corner; milkweed spreads aggressively by rhizome.
entries.source: Xerces Society monarch and pollinator guide

entries.description: Butterfly weed (Asclepias tuberosa) is a clumping non-rhizomatous milkweed that draws monarchs and supports a wide native pollinator base. Plant at row ends as a non-aggressive perennial companion to tomato beds.
entries.source: Xerces Society monarch and pollinator guide; USDA-NRCS

entries.description: Swamp milkweed (Asclepias incarnata) is a moisture-tolerant clumping milkweed; planting at the edge of squash beds with overhead irrigation supplies pollinator habitat and monarch host without dryland milkweed's aggressive spread.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Black chokeberry (aronia) blooms in May overlapping the blueberry bloom window, supporting native bee and bumblebee populations across both crops. Both tolerate acidic soil (pH 4.5-6.0); a natural berry-row hedge.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Xerces Society

entries.description: Serviceberry (Amelanchier) blooms in early spring overlapping apple bloom, supporting native pollinators when honeybee activity is still light. Multi-functional: pollinator hub, edible fruit, and orchard windbreak.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Elderberry (Sambucus) blooms June-July when apple pest pressure builds; the flat umbel flowers draw predatory wasps that target codling moth larvae. Plant as orchard hedgerow for combined pollinator and predator habitat.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list; Xerces Society

entries.description: Pawpaw flowers are carrion-pollinated (flies, beetles) rather than bee-pollinated; elderberry adjacent supports the broader-spectrum pollinator and predator community without competing for pawpaw's specific pollinators.
entries.source: Kentucky State University pawpaw research; USDA-NRCS

entries.description: Comfrey under pawpaw trees suppresses competing turf and supplies a slow K and P release. Pawpaw shallow feeder roots benefit from the moisture-retaining mulch in mid-summer dry spells.
entries.source: Kentucky State University pawpaw research; Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: New England aster bloom in September-October extends pollinator habitat past tomato harvest into late-season fruit ripening. Supports late-flying native bees that overwinter in nearby leaf litter; valuable for permanent garden edges.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: New England aster supports fall-flying pollinators and parasitic wasps that target late-season cabbage worm. Plant at edge of brassica beds for permanent late-bloom habitat.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: New England aster blooms during apple harvest, supporting overwintering native bee populations for the following year's pollination. Plant in orchard alley ends as permanent fall habitat.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Blazing star (Liatris) supplies a continuous late-summer bloom that supports parasitic wasps and butterflies. The clumping perennial habit fits at brassica bed corners without spreading.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: Liatris supports parasitic wasps that target tomato hornworm and aphids. The 3-4 ft flower spike also provides a habitat perch for predatory wasps and assassin bugs.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: Tropical sage (Salvia coccinea) supplies hummingbird and bumblebee habitat through the pepper bloom and fruiting window. Bumblebees boost pepper fruit set rates through buzz pollination on the small pepper flowers.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as pepper: tropical sage draws bumblebees that improve eggplant fruit set through buzz pollination. Border eggplant beds with salvia clumps for continuous habitat.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Lemon bee balm (Monarda citriodora) draws bumblebees and honeybees during the cucumber flowering window. The strong citrus aroma also deters cucumber beetle in proximity plantings.
entries.source: Xerces Society; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as cucumber: lemon bee balm supplies pollinator habitat for cucurbit beds. The lemon aroma reduces squash bug oviposition pressure when planted at row ends.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Black-eyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta) blooms continuously from July through frost, supporting hoverflies and parasitic wasps that target tomato hornworm. Native and drought-tolerant; an easy permanent border.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: Rudbeckia supports the parasitic wasp and hoverfly community attacking cabbage worm and aphids. Self-seeding native perennial fits brassica bed borders.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: Tickseed (Coreopsis) blooms from June through frost, supplying continuous hoverfly and parasitic wasp habitat. Drought-tolerant native; pairs well with permanent garden-edge plantings near tomato beds.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Russian comfrey Bocking 14 cultivar is sterile (no self-seeding) and the most popular orchard guild companion. Deep taproot mines subsoil K and P; foliage chopped 3-4 times per season supplies high-K mulch.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2; Yeomans, Water for Every Farm

entries.description: Same mechanism as apple: sterile Russian comfrey at pear root zone supplies K and P mulch without escaping into the orchard floor. The preferred comfrey cultivar for commercial orchard guild plantings.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism: sterile Bocking 14 comfrey under peach trees recycles potassium for stone fruit production. Stone fruit's high K demand benefits from the slow continuous release.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism: sterile Bocking 14 cultivar suits cherry guild plantings without spreading. Most reliable comfrey variant for permanent orchard floor design.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Comfrey at asparagus bed corners mines subsoil minerals that asparagus's deep crown system also accesses. Chop-and-drop foliage provides a slow K release timed to spear emergence.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Comfrey adjacent to rhubarb mines K and P for rhubarb's heavy nutrient demand. Both are long-lived perennials; pair them in a permanent perennial bed for low-maintenance soil-building.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

entries.description: Comfrey leaves applied as tea or chopped mulch around tomato plants supply potassium for fruit development. The slow-release nutrient profile reduces blossom-end rot in calcium-stressed beds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Toensmeier, Perennial Vegetables

entries.description: Yarrow at brassica bed corners draws parasitic wasps and hoverflies that target cabbage worm and aphids. Drought-tolerant perennial fits well at row ends with reduced mowing.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Yarrow patches near pepper rows support parasitic wasps that attack pepper aphid and pepper maggot. Drought-tolerant perennial that thrives in low-input garden edges.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Yarrow near bean rows draws beneficial insect populations that prey on bean leaf beetle and Mexican bean beetle. Plant in permanent row-end clumps for continuous predator support.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Winter wheat following an alfalfa stand of 3+ years receives 100-150 lb residual N/acre. The standard wheat-alfalfa rotation in the central US; alfalfa builds soil structure and N reserves before grain.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; Iowa State Extension

entries.description: Corn following a 3-4 year alfalfa stand receives 150-250 lb residual N/acre plus organic matter from heavy root mass. The classic alfalfa-corn rotation eliminates synthetic N for the first year after alfalfa termination.
entries.source: Iowa State Extension; USDA-ARS rotation research

entries.description: Sainfoin (Onobrychis viciifolia) is a dryland perennial legume that builds soil N and tilth before grain sorghum. Drought tolerance suits arid and semi-arid regions where alfalfa establishment is unreliable.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Montana State Extension

entries.description: Birdsfoot trefoil mixed with timothy in hay or pasture stands fixes 50-150 lb N/acre and provides bloat-resistant forage (condensed tannins prevent ruminant bloat). Standard cool-season pasture pair.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Conservation Practice Standards; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Forage chicory's deep taproot opens compacted soil for alfalfa roots and brings up minerals from subsoil. The combination supplies high-protein forage with anti-parasitic properties (condensed tannins).
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Conservation Practice Standards; OSU Extension

entries.description: Red clover and timothy hay mixtures combine N fixation with grass biomass. Clover supplies 80-150 lb N/acre to the grass stand; the pair is the standard cool-climate hay mix in the northeastern US.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Conservation Practice Standards; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: White clover in perennial ryegrass pastures fixes 100-200 lb N/acre and improves protein content. Standard temperate-pasture pair in the British Isles, Pacific Northwest, and northern Atlantic US.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Conservation Practice Standards

entries.description: Subterranean clover is a self-seeding annual legume that fits tall fescue pastures in Mediterranean and humid subtropical zones. Buried seed pods germinate after summer dormancy; pair fixes 50-120 lb N/acre annually.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; UC ANR pasture management

entries.description: Orchardgrass mixed with alfalfa supplies persistent cool-season grass biomass; alfalfa fixes 100-300 lb N/acre per year that supports grass productivity. Standard dairy-quality forage mix in the upper Midwest.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; Penn State Extension forage

entries.description: Italian Romano flat-podded bush bean planted between tomato rows fixes N during early tomato season. Harvest beans before tomato canopy fully shades the bed; the bean residue supplies post-harvest N to fall tomato fruiting.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Romano-type pole beans co-cropped with winter squash in a three-sisters variant supply nitrogen while bean foliage threads through squash vines. Italian heirloom variant of the classic indigenous pattern.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS Three Sisters research

entries.description: Pole Romano bean trellised at tomato row ends fixes N without shading the tomato canopy. Vertical bean structure separates root zones for shared bed efficiency, complementing bush Romano variants in the same bed.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Roma II bush bean rows adjacent to spring cabbage supply N during cabbage head formation. Bean harvest is complete before cabbage matures, releasing bed space and residue N for the cabbage finish.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Italian Romano pole bean climbs sweet corn stalks in a three-sisters variant. Romano's flat-pod habit and tender harvest make it a preferred kitchen-garden choice over standard pole beans.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS Three Sisters research

entries.description: Korean mint (Agastache rugosa) bloom extends from June through October, supporting bumblebees and parasitic wasps targeting tomato hornworm. Aromatic foliage also deters some flea beetle activity.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Anise hyssop's continuous July-October bloom supports an exceptionally diverse native bee community plus parasitic wasps. Drought-tolerant perennial; pairs well with permanent garden-edge borders.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: anise hyssop supports the parasitic wasp and hoverfly community attacking cabbage worm. Permanent perennial fits brassica bed border designs.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Anise hyssop in orchard alley supports a continuous pollinator habitat from June through frost. Particularly valuable for late-season native-bee buildup that overwinters in adjacent hedgerow.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Fennel anethole and related volatiles inhibit lettuce seedling growth in proximity plantings. Maintain at least 4 ft separation from lettuce beds; fennel root exudates persist after the plant is removed.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Fennel allelopathy suppresses spinach germination and early growth. Same 4 ft separation rule applies; do not rotate fennel directly to spinach beds.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Same mechanism as bush bean: fennel anethole inhibits lima bean germination and Rhizobium nodulation. Maintain at least 6 ft separation between fennel and any leguminous bean.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Fennel anethole inhibits fava bean germination and root development. Plant fava beans well away from fennel beds; rotation order matters for the following season as well.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Edamame soybean shares the same Rhizobium-disruption sensitivity as other legumes. Fennel allelopathy reduces edamame yield in close plantings; maintain a minimum 6 ft separation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Same mechanism: fennel anethole suppresses chickpea germination and nodulation. Maintain bed separation; rotate chickpea to a fennel-free bed for the year following fennel.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Peanut nodulation is suppressed by fennel root exudates in adjacent plantings. Plant peanuts in separate beds from fennel; allelopathy persists for one season after fennel is removed.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Cucumber within the black walnut root zone shows juglone wilt and stunted growth. Maintain at least 50 ft separation; cucumber tolerates juglone less well than most cucurbits.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; OSU Extension

entries.description: Lettuce within the black walnut root zone wilts under juglone exposure. Locate lettuce beds upslope of mature walnut trees or maintain at least 50 ft buffer.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Rhubarb under black walnut shows crown decline and reduced stalk production over multiple seasons. Plant rhubarb crowns at least 50 ft from any mature black walnut.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Strawberry runners and crowns are juglone-sensitive; plantings within 50 ft of mature black walnut show reduced fruit set and crown dieback. Avoid both above-ground and below-ground proximity.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; OSU Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard raspberry: Heritage primocane-fruiting raspberry is juglone-sensitive. Maintain a 70 ft buffer from any mature black walnut tree.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Jersey highbush blueberry shares the family-wide juglone sensitivity. Maintain at least 70 ft buffer from any mature black walnut; symptoms include slow growth and reduced berry production.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; OSU Extension

entries.description: Savoy cabbage within the English walnut root zone shows yellowing and slow growth. Maintain a 40 ft buffer to keep brassica beds productive near established walnut trees.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Red cabbage shares the brassica-family juglone sensitivity. Plant at least 40 ft from English walnut; symptoms include yellowing leaves and reduced head formation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Strawberry crowns within 40 ft of English walnut show reduced fruit set. The toxic root zone of English walnut is smaller than black walnut's but still encompasses several decades of soil juglone accumulation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Pepper within the pecan root zone (30-40 ft from mature trunks) shows mild juglone exposure symptoms: leaf wilt, reduced fruit set. Plant pepper beds at least 50 ft from mature pecan.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Eggplant in the pecan root zone shows mild juglone wilt. Plant eggplant beds a minimum of 40 ft from mature pecan trees; nightshades are the most juglone-sensitive vegetable family.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Potato in the pecan root zone shows vine wilt and reduced tuber development. Plant potato beds outside the pecan dripline at minimum, ideally 40 ft from mature trees.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Blueberry roots in pecan-influenced soil exhibit weakened crown vigor over multiple seasons. Plant blueberry beds well outside the pecan root zone; soil pH (pecan prefers neutral, blueberry prefers acidic) also separates the species.
entries.source: USDA-ARS juglone research

entries.description: Reverse mechanism: tomato canopy and root competition further limit fennel growth, while fennel's allelopathic effect on tomato is established. Maintain at least 6 ft separation between fennel and tomato beds.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Reverse-direction note: lettuce avoids fennel root zones. Plant fennel in a separate bed; fennel residue persists in the soil for one season after harvest.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Hot pepper and potato share Verticillium wilt and aphid-vectored viruses. Mixed nightshade beds amplify disease pressure; rotate apart on a 3-year cycle and maintain at least 20 ft separation between rows.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC IPM

entries.description: Eggplant and potato share Verticillium wilt and several aphid-vectored viruses. Maintain separation in the same bed; do not rotate one immediately following the other.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC IPM

entries.description: Pepper and potato share several nightshade-family pathogens including Phytophthora and aphid-vectored viruses. Plant in separate beds; rotate apart on a 3-year cycle.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Cucumber and squash share several cucurbit pathogens (downy mildew, anthracnose, mosaic viruses) and host the same cucumber beetle vector. Maintain physical separation; rotate apart for disease control.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Cucumber and cantaloupe share aphid-vectored viruses including cucurbit yellow stunting disorder. Avoid interplanting in the same bed; both crops are highly susceptible to the same vector pressure.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as cantaloupe: watermelon and cucumber share virus vectors. Separate cucurbit families across beds; do not rotate one directly into the other's residue.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Celery's slow growth and shallow root system fit between lettuce rows during cool-season cropping. Lettuce shade reduces celery bolting pressure during warm spring days; harvest lettuce before celery hits 12 inches.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Leek between carrot rows confuses carrot rust fly with sulfur volatiles, and carrot bed humidity supports leek growth without direct competition. The classic French intensive vegetable pairing.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

entries.description: Same mechanism as leek: shallot sulfur volatiles deter carrot rust fly oviposition. Shallot's smaller stature fits more easily between carrot rows than leek.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Music garlic's tall scape and strong sulfur volatiles deter carrot rust fly when interplanted at carrot bed edges. Harvest garlic before carrot canopy closes for shared bed efficiency.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as music garlic: rocambole-type hardneck garlic provides strong sulfur volatile cover near carrot beds. Especially suited to cold-climate carrot rotations.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Music garlic interplanted along tomato rows deters whitefly and spider mite through sulfur volatile diffusion. Harvest garlic by July before tomato canopy fully shades the bed.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard garlic: purple-stripe hardneck garlic in a rose-bed ring reduces blackspot and aphid pressure. The hardneck variety produces scapes that double as cut flowers.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Garlic chive (Allium tuberosum) sulfur volatiles deter cabbage aphid and reduce cabbage moth oviposition. Perennial habit means continuous coverage; small clumping form fits easily in brassica bed corners.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Snapdragon (Antirrhinum majus) flowers are bumblebee-pollinated and supply habitat through the early tomato bloom window. Border tomato beds with snapdragon for color plus pollinator support.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Larkspur (Delphinium consolida) draws bumblebees and large native bees during the early tomato fruiting window. Plant at the bed edge; larkspur self-seeds in tilled gardens.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Love-in-a-mist (Nigella damascena) supplies a cool-season pollinator habitat that overlaps cucumber's early flowering. Self-seeding annual; pairs well with permanent-bed designs.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: May Night salvia's deep-purple spikes support bumblebees through the May-June tomato planting and early bloom window. Perennial habit fits permanent garden-edge borders near vegetable beds.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Wood sage (Salvia nemorosa) blooms repeatedly through summer with deadheading, supporting bumblebee populations across tomato fruit set and ripening. Drought-tolerant perennial.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: Bonita French marigold (Tagetes patula) produces high concentrations of alpha-terthienyl, suppressing root-knot nematode in surrounding root zones. Plant continuously between tomato rows for one full season for measurable nematode reduction.
entries.source: USDA-ARS root-knot nematode research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: Bonita French marigold suppresses root-knot nematode in pepper beds. Continuous row planting between pepper rows yields season-long nematode reduction.
entries.source: USDA-ARS root-knot nematode research

entries.description: Crackerjack African marigold (Tagetes erecta) at row ends suppresses root-knot nematode and discourages cucumber beetle scouting. The 30 inch height also forms a visible bed-edge marker that supports orderly cultivation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS root-knot nematode research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Crackerjack marigold border on summer squash beds repels cucumber beetle and squash bug while suppressing root-knot nematode through the season.
entries.source: USDA-ARS root-knot nematode research

entries.description: Sparky French marigold (compact bi-color cultivar) repels whitefly and aphids and supplies a bright continuous edge for tomato rows. Smaller habit than Bonita; fits in cage-row gaps without crowding.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS

entries.description: Tagetes-genus marigolds in brassica bed corners suppress root-knot nematode and discourage cabbage moth oviposition through volatile signal interference. Plant continuously for one full season to achieve nematode suppression.
entries.source: USDA-ARS nematode research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: marigolds at cabbage bed edges deter cabbage moth and suppress soil nematodes. Combine with sweet alyssum borders for hoverfly populations.
entries.source: USDA-ARS nematode research

entries.description: Marigolds in carrot bed corners suppress soil nematodes during the long carrot growing season. Particularly valuable on warm-climate carrot beds where root-knot pressure builds quickly.
entries.source: USDA-ARS nematode research; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Fuji apple's late-season ripening benefits from white-clover N supply through August fruit fill. Permanent low-mow clover floor supplies steady N without competing for water in the critical August window.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; OSU Extension

entries.description: Gala matures in late August through September overlapping the second-flight codling moth window. Tansy clumps at the tree base release pyrethrin-related volatiles that reduce moth oviposition during fruit ripening.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.description: Granny Smith's exceptionally late harvest (October-November) benefits from late-season N supply. White-clover orchard floor releases N steadily through fall fruit fill; mow alternate strips to maintain bloom.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Mullein's tall flowering spike and basal rosette form a vertical predator perch and pollinator habitat in Honeycrisp orchard rows. Particularly valuable for biennial-flowering predator perch rotation through the dwarfing rootstock blocks.
entries.source: University of Minnesota Honeycrisp production notes; Xerces Society

entries.description: Gravenstein's heritage hardiness and shallow feeder roots benefit from comfrey's moisture-retaining mulch and subsoil mineral mining. Plant a ring at the dripline of mature trees.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism as Gravenstein: McIntosh's heritage form benefits from comfrey root-zone mulch. Particularly valuable in colder zones where shallow McIntosh roots need insulation.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Cosmic Crisp's late October harvest extends past second-flight codling moth pressure. Tansy clumps at tree bases reduce moth oviposition during the final long ripening window unique to this WSU cultivar.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.description: Sweet alyssum continuous border supports the hoverfly population that WSU trials identified as a key aphid predator for Cosmic Crisp orchards. Plant at row ends for season-long predator support.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.description: Same mechanism as Bartlett: Red Bartlett shares root habit and benefits from comfrey ring at dripline. Color sport of Bartlett with identical cultural requirements.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Hosui Asian pear benefits from comfrey root-zone mulch and slow K release. The Asian pear's relatively shallow feeder roots and high crop load need balanced potassium supply.
entries.source: OSU Extension Asian pear production; Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard peach: Redhaven (the most-planted commercial peach in the US) benefits from a daffodil collar at the dripline for vole and gopher prevention through winter dormancy.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Tree Fruit Production Guide

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard peach: Elberta heirloom (the dominant US peach of the 20th century) benefits from chive volatiles deterring peach tree borer and confusing oriental fruit moth oviposition.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension peach production

entries.description: Contender peach's exceptional cold hardiness suits cold-climate orchards where vole pressure is heaviest under deep mulch and snowpack. Daffodil collar is a reliable winter barrier.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Tree Fruit

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard sweet cherry: chive volatiles deter cherry fruit fly and reduce slug damage on low fruit. Bing's heavy cropping habit makes preventive pest control especially valuable.
entries.source: OSU Extension cherry production

entries.description: Rainier cherry's yellow-blush skin is exceptionally bird-attractive; chive ring at the trunk supplies typical sulfur deterrents while overhead netting handles bird pressure separately.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard sour cherry: Montmorency's compact tree and heavy crop benefit from comfrey K-cycling mulch. The dominant US tart cherry; this pairing supports backyard and commercial guild plantings.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2; Michigan State Extension

entries.description: Stella self-fertile sweet cherry suits single-tree backyard plantings; a chive ring at the base supplies sulfur volatiles deterring borer and slug. Especially useful for small-space orchard plantings.
entries.source: OSU Extension cherry production

entries.description: Italian plum (prune-type) chive ring at the trunk deters plum curculio scouting. The European-prune type benefits from layered pest control through fruit ripening.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Tree Fruit

entries.description: Santa Rosa Japanese plum is particularly attractive to Japanese beetle; tansy clumps at the tree base release pyrethrin-related volatiles that reduce beetle feeding pressure during fruit set.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

entries.description: Damson plum's smaller stature and heavy cropping habit benefit from comfrey K and P cycling at the dripline. Damson trees are typically grown in tight spacings; comfrey ring fits without competing for light.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard apricot: Blenheim heirloom benefits from chive sulfur volatiles deterring borer. The dominant California apricot; matches mediterranean-climate orchard guild design.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener stone fruit

entries.description: Tilton apricot's vigorous growth and prolific cropping benefit from daffodil collar protection through winter dormancy. Heritage California apricot; particularly suited to mediterranean-climate plantings.
entries.source: UC ANR; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard nectarine: Fantasia's vigorous tree and heavy fruit load benefit from a daffodil collar protecting against winter vole damage. Particularly relevant for orchards with mulched soil floors.
entries.source: Penn State Extension Tree Fruit; UC ANR

entries.description: Jefferson hazelnut wood and live tree shade make ideal substrate and placement for shiitake. Eastern Filbert Blight resistance makes Jefferson the leading PNW commercial hazelnut; the coppice produces shiitake-grade wood.
entries.source: OSU Extension forest products; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.description: Same mechanism as Jefferson: Yamhill is another EFB-resistant OSU hazelnut release suitable for shiitake substrate and understory log placement.
entries.source: OSU Extension forest products

entries.description: Hazelnut wood is excellent substrate for lion's mane (Hericium erinaceus); inoculated logs fruit reliably in shaded coppice plantings. Pair with shiitake to fill complementary mushroom production windows.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Sugar maple makes excellent lion's mane substrate; logs cut during winter dormancy and inoculated in spring fruit in fall. Pair with shiitake-log production for complementary fruiting windows.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; University of Vermont Extension

entries.description: Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) fruits well on sugar maple stump and log substrate. Slow primary decomposer; produces a multi-year fruiting cycle on a single inoculated log.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: King oyster (Pleurotus eryngii) fruits well on alder substrate. Red alder coppice rotations supply continuous fresh substrate; growers in the PNW use alder for multiple oyster species in rotation.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; OSU Extension forest products

entries.description: Nameko (Pholiota microspora) fruits on alder logs in cool autumn conditions. Particularly suited to PNW maritime climates where the alder grows naturally and nameko fruiting is reliable.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Pioppino (Agrocybe aegerita) fruits well on hazelnut wood. Italian traditional cultivation pairs hazelnut coppice with pioppino logs for a high-value mushroom and nut combination.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.description: Amistad salvia's prolific deep-purple spikes draw hummingbirds and bumblebees through summer and into fall. Pair with tomato beds for hummingbird-aided pollinator activity around tomato bloom.
entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Orange-flowered Agastache (A. aurantiaca) draws hummingbirds and bumblebees continuously from June to October. Hummingbird-pollinated salvias and agastaches form a layered pollinator hub near vegetable beds.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator hedgerow plant list

entries.description: Pincushion flower (Scabiosa) is a top pollinator attractor for butterflies, bumblebees, and hoverflies. Border tomato beds with scabiosa for continuous pollinator and predator habitat.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: scabiosa supports hoverfly populations attacking cabbage aphid. Continuous bloom from June through frost pairs well with succession brassica plantings.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard borage: White Alba borage cultivar offers the same prolific bee-attracting flowers in white. Color variety supports gardens designed around a specific palette without losing pollinator function.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: White Alba borage near strawberry beds supports bumblebee and honeybee populations during runner-set and fruit-set windows. Same mechanism as standard blue borage with color variation.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS pollinator research

entries.description: Sulfur cosmos draws honeybees, bumblebees, and parasitic wasps through the full tomato season. More heat-tolerant than standard cosmos; pairs well with warm-climate tomato production.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: sulfur cosmos supports parasitic wasps attacking cabbage worm. Heat-tolerant; particularly useful for summer brassica beds in southern climates.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Sulfur cosmos at cucumber row ends supplies pollinator habitat throughout the cucumber bloom window. Reseeds itself easily in warm-climate gardens.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Chocolate cosmos (Cosmos atrosanguineus) draws bumblebees and supports the long winter squash bloom and ripening window. Less heat-tolerant than sulfur cosmos but supplies darker color in mixed pollinator gardens.
entries.source: Xerces Society

entries.description: State Fair Zinnia is the tallest cultivar (30-40 inches), forming a back-row pollinator hedge for tomato beds. Long-stem cut-flower form supplies pollinator habitat plus indoor bouquet harvests.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Benary Giant zinnia (the most popular cut-flower zinnia in commercial production) supplies long-bloom butterfly and bumblebee habitat in tomato beds. Tall habit pairs with tomato cages without crowding low-canopy lettuce or basil interplants.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Zahara zinnia's compact 10-12 inch habit fits brassica bed borders without shading low brassicas. Powdery mildew resistant; outperforms standard zinnias in humid climates.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Mexican sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) draws monarchs, swallowtails, and bumblebees at heights up to 6 ft. Plant at back of tomato beds; tithonia's tall habit fits behind tomato cages and trellises.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: tithonia supports a diverse pollinator and predator community across the cucumber season. The tall habit fits at trellis ends without shading cucumber vines.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

entries.description: Flossflower (Ageratum) supplies a low (6-12 inch) continuous-bloom edge for tomato bed borders. Draws hoverflies and parasitic wasps targeting tomato hornworm and aphids.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: ageratum border supports parasitic wasp habitat near brassica beds. Compact form fits between tall brassicas without shading.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Globe amaranth (Gomphrena globosa) supports butterflies and bumblebees through the full tomato season. Heat and drought tolerant; pairs well with warm-climate tomato beds.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Yarrow at raspberry row ends draws parasitic wasps and hoverflies that target raspberry aphid and raspberry cane borer. Drought-tolerant perennial fits between row passes without supplemental watering.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Xerces Society

entries.description: Same mechanism as raspberry: yarrow at blueberry row corners supports parasitic wasp habitat targeting fruit-fly pressure. Tolerates the acidic blueberry soil better than most pollinator perennials.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Xerces Society

entries.description: Yarrow at blackberry row corners supplies parasitoid habitat for raspberry crown borer and bramble cane borer management. Drought-tolerant perennial pairs well with mulched bramble rows.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Blueberry and lingonberry share Ericaceae family soil preferences (acidic pH 4.5-5.5) and benefit from interplanting in a permanent acidic-soil bed. Lingonberry's low evergreen mat suppresses weeds and bears its own fruit crop.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard strawberry: Albion day-neutral cultivar produces fruit through summer and benefits from borage's continuous pollinator support. Particularly valuable for everbearing strawberry production.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; USDA-ARS

entries.description: Everbearing strawberries fruit twice (spring and fall); borage supports pollinators across both fruiting windows. Self-seeding annual ensures continuous coverage.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard strawberry: Seascape day-neutral benefits from creeping thyme as a slug-suppressing living mulch. Common pairing in PNW commercial production where Seascape is the standard cultivar.
entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops

entries.description: Buckwheat sown between kale plantings serves as a fast pollinator and beneficial-insect insectary. The 6-week cycle clears the bed for fall kale seeding while building hoverfly populations.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard kale: Redbor's deep red color and long growing window pair well with buckwheat sown at row ends. Continuous flowering through summer supports the parasitoid community attacking aphids.
entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Buckwheat between collard plantings serves the same fast-rotation pollinator-supporting role as in kale beds. Particularly suited to southern US collard production where buckwheat thrives in summer.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; SARE Cover Crops field guide

entries.description: Sweet alyssum borders on lettuce beds support hoverfly populations whose larvae prey on lettuce aphid. Low-growing habit fits between lettuce rows; tolerates cool-season conditions.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR

entries.description: Same mechanism as lettuce: sweet alyssum at spinach bed borders supports hoverflies that target spinach aphid. Both crops are cool-season; alyssum tolerates the same conditions.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Sweet alyssum borders on carrot beds support hoverfly populations whose larvae prey on carrot aphid. The low habit fits naturally as a cool-season carrot bed edge.
entries.source: UC ANR; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Sweet alyssum at tomato bed borders supports hoverflies and parasitic wasps targeting tomato hornworm, aphids, and whitefly. The low-growing habit fits in front of taller marigolds and basil for layered pollinator edges.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Xerces Society
