entries.description: Sweet alyssum at brassica bed borders supports hoverflies whose larvae prey on cabbage aphid. UC ANR trials documented measurable aphid reduction in alyssum-bordered brassica beds.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as broccoli: sweet alyssum supports parasitoid populations attacking cabbage aphid and cabbage worm. Continuous border planting maintains predator habitat all season.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener

entries.description: Amaranth grain at tomato bed edges traps flea beetles and serves as a host for tarnished plant bug, drawing pressure away from tomato fruits. Grain harvest at season end recovers the crop's primary value.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as tomato: amaranth grain traps flea beetles drawn to kale's mustard volatiles. Harvest amaranth at season end for grain; kale benefits from reduced flea beetle pressure during head development.
entries.source: USDA-ARS

entries.description: Purple Prince amaranth is a tall (5-7 ft) ornamental-edible variety useful as a flea beetle trap and visual back-of-bed edging for tomato rows. Edible greens and seed head also recover food value.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Native Seeds/SEARCH

entries.description: Amaranth grain at brassica bed edges traps flea beetles and supplies an end-of-season grain harvest. Pair with sweet alyssum border for combined predator support.
entries.source: USDA-ARS

entries.description: Amaranth grain near pepper rows traps tarnished plant bug, reducing pressure on pepper fruits. The tall habit also supports trellised pepper rows by acting as a windbreak.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Horseradish at potato bed corners releases allyl isothiocyanate that suppresses Colorado potato beetle scouting and discourages potato scab pathogen activity in adjacent soil. Contain with deep edging.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.description: Horseradish at tomato bed corners suppresses some soilborne fungal pathogens (Verticillium, Fusarium) via allyl isothiocyanate root exudates. Plant in a buried-sided container to prevent spread into the bed.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; USDA-ARS biofumigation research

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard apple: Northern Spy heritage cultivar benefits from comfrey K and P cycling at the dripline. Long-lived heritage tree (40+ year crops); the permanent guild supports decades of production.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Same mechanism: Ribston Pippin heritage cider cultivar benefits from comfrey root-zone cycling. Heritage cider apples typically thrive in low-input orchard floor designs with permanent guild plantings.
entries.source: Jacke and Toensmeier, Edible Forest Gardens v2

entries.description: Heritage Newtown Pippin's long October-November harvest window benefits from white-clover N supply through fall fruit fill. Particularly valuable for cider production seeking late-season aroma development.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Same mechanism: heritage Roxbury Russet (the oldest American apple cultivar) benefits from white-clover N supply through its long storage-apple finish. Cider and dessert use; permanent guild design is the historic norm.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Heritage Harrison cider apple thrives in low-input orchard floor designs with white-clover N supply. Bittersweet cider cultivar; the heritage growing system is the canonical pairing.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

entries.description: Fiber-type milkweed (Asclepias syriaca cultivar) supplies the same pollinator and monarch host function as common milkweed with somewhat reduced rhizome spread. Plant in a contained bed corner near tomato.
entries.source: USDA-NRCS; Xerces Society

entries.description: Flat-leaf parsley between asparagus rows deters asparagus beetle through volatile masking. Parsley's tap root and crown coexist with asparagus's deep root system without competition.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Same mechanism as flat parsley: curly parsley between asparagus rows masks crop volatiles deterring asparagus beetle. Curly form is more cold-hardy than flat parsley in northern asparagus beds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Basil between asparagus rows deters asparagus beetle through eugenol and linalool volatiles. Tradition holds basil also enhances asparagus flavor in adjacent harvests, though the flavor mechanism is unconfirmed.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Riotte, Carrots Love Tomatoes

entries.description: Rosemary's pinene and cineole volatiles deter carrot rust fly when planted at carrot bed edges. The perennial habit suits permanent garden-edge plantings adjacent to seasonal carrot rotations.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Sage at carrot bed edges deters carrot rust fly and supplies pollinator habitat during the spring carrot rust fly emergence window. The perennial habit fits permanent garden borders.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Lemon balm flowers draw honeybees and bumblebees during the long tomato bloom window. Aromatic foliage also deters whitefly when handled (volatile release). Contain spread; lemon balm self-seeds aggressively.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Cunningham, Great Garden Companions

entries.description: Lemon verbena's continuous summer bloom supports pollinators and supplies a fragrant herb harvest at tomato bed corners. Tender perennial; grow in containers in cold zones for season extension.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Lemongrass citral and geraniol repel whitefly, aphids, and mosquitoes around tomato beds. Tropical perennial; grow in pots in cold zones, in-ground in zones 9+.
entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Potato in fields following winter rye shows reduced sprout vigor due to BX-compound residue. Allow at least 4 weeks between rye incorporation and potato planting; or rotate through a buckwheat buffer.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research; OSU Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard lettuce: Buttercrunch is rye-sensitive; allow 3+ weeks after rye mow-down before direct seeding. Transplanting Buttercrunch starts bypasses the germination-phase sensitivity.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Same mechanism as leaf lettuce: Romaine seedlings show suppressed growth in rye-residue soil. Allow 3+ weeks between rye and Romaine direct seeding.
entries.source: USDA-ARS allelopathy research

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard carrot: Nantes carrots interplanted with leek confuse carrot rust fly through sulfur volatile masking. Nantes's mild flavor and sweet character are unaffected by allium proximity.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

entries.description: Same mechanism: Danvers heritage carrot benefits from leek interplanting for rust fly deterrence. Danvers's heavy yield and storage character pair well with succession leek harvests.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Imperator long-rooted carrots benefit from leek interplanting. Imperator's deep root requires loose soil that leek tolerates well in shared beds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard cabbage: Savoy cultivar pairs with sweet alyssum border for hoverfly habitat. The crinkled-leaf Savoy benefits especially from aphid suppression as aphids hide in leaf folds.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener

entries.description: Same mechanism: red cabbage pairs with sweet alyssum for hoverfly support. Red cabbage's anthocyanin pigments deter aphid feeding slightly but alyssum's predator support remains valuable.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener

entries.description: Same mechanism: broccoli raab pairs with sweet alyssum for hoverfly habitat during the spring or fall short cycle. Broccoli raab's fast bolt means alyssum's continuous bloom complements early harvest.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener

entries.description: Same mechanism: overwintered Purple Sprouting Broccoli benefits from spring alyssum borders that flower during the broccoli flush. Continuous predator habitat supports the long overwintered crop cycle.
entries.source: OSU Extension; UC ANR

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard onion-carrot pairing: Walla Walla sweet onion and Nantes carrot in shared beds deter rust fly and onion maggot through reciprocal volatile masking. Both varieties tolerate the Pacific Northwest's cool spring.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit and Vegetable Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: yellow storage onion interplanted with Danvers carrot supports long-storage harvests of both crops. Mutual pest deterrence supports a tight rotation without synthetic inputs.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Red Tropea Italian onion paired with Imperator carrot supports the mediterranean kitchen-garden combination with reciprocal pest deterrence.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Tomato vines shade carrot beds during midsummer, slowing carrot bolting and reducing soil moisture loss. Plant carrots in front of tomato cages during midsummer; harvest carrots through fall as tomato vines decline.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

entries.description: Tomato vines shade lettuce beds, extending the spring lettuce window into early summer by reducing bolt pressure. Plant lettuce on the east side of tomato cages for morning sun and afternoon shade.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

entries.description: Same mechanism as lettuce: tomato vines shade spinach beds, extending the spring spinach window. Spinach tolerates more shade than lettuce; plant under tomato cages through summer for continuous baby leaf harvest.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: tomato vines shade arugula beds, slowing bolt and extending the arugula harvest window into summer. Arugula tolerates more heat than lettuce or spinach but still benefits from canopy cover.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as standard potato-marigold pairing: Yukon Gold benefits from marigold border deterring Colorado potato beetle and root nematode. Yukon's storage character and early-mid maturity suits the marigold-protected bed.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Red Norland early new-potato variety pairs with marigold border for beetle deterrence during the short early-spring window. New potato harvest before peak beetle pressure supports the rotation.
entries.source: USDA-ARS; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Potato onion (Allium cepa Aggregatum group) at tomato bed edges supplies the same sulfur volatile deterrence as standard onion while clumping into multiplier bulbs for next-year replanting.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: potato onion (multiplier onion) between carrot rows deters rust fly through sulfur volatiles. The clumping habit supplies onions for sequential harvest through the carrot season.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Egyptian walking onion (top-setting variety) supplies year-round green onion harvest and continuous sulfur volatile cover near tomato beds. Self-propagating; plant once and harvest indefinitely.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Egyptian walking onion in permanent clumps near carrot beds deters carrot rust fly. The perennial habit suits permanent garden-edge designs adjacent to seasonal carrot rotations.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Borage at cucumber bed edges draws bumblebees and honeybees during the cucumber bloom window, improving female-flower fruit set rates. Cucumber is moderately pollinator-limited; borage materially improves harvest.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism as cucumber: borage near squash beds draws bumblebees that improve squash fruit set. Bumblebees are the most effective squash pollinators; borage forms a high-density bumblebee station.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research

entries.description: Same mechanism: borage near summer squash and zucchini supplies high-density bumblebee habitat that supports the heavy fruit set demand. Plant a borage clump every 8-10 ft along squash rows.
entries.source: USDA-ARS pollinator research

entries.description: Borage at kale bed corners supports pollinator habitat during kale bolt and seed set. The flower also draws hoverflies whose larvae prey on aphids hiding in kale leaf folds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Hardneck purple-stripe garlic in a ring around rose roots reduces blackspot fungus and aphid colonization. The hardneck scapes also supply early-summer cut-flower harvest before garlic bulbs mature.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.description: Same mechanism: Music porcelain-type hardneck garlic in a rose-bed ring supplies long-storage garlic bulbs and continuous sulfur volatile cover. The scapes form a soft-purple flower head valued in cut arrangements.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension
